Sector Lab v3 PUNCHLINE — Back-test

Run date: 2026-04-22 · Hypothesis test on whether the fast/slow composite partition adds information beyond the existing blended COMP as a leading indicator for tactical (60d) and strategic (252d) S&P drawdowns + cross-sectional sector rotation.
Data: 25 macro indicators from indicator_history.json · S&P 500 + 11 SPDR sector ETFs from Yahoo · Coverage 2006-01 to 2026-04 (slow composite degraded pre-2011).

Verdict — the v3 PUNCHLINE framework as calibrated does NOT work as a leading indicator

  • Fast composite does NOT add information beyond current COMP on tactical (60d) horizon.
    Fast AUC = 0.558 vs COMP AUC = 0.558 — tied. Splitting fast/slow buys nothing on this horizon.
  • Fast composite is BEATEN by VIX alone on tactical horizon.
    Fast AUC = 0.558 vs VIX-alone AUC = 0.590. A single z-scored VIX is more predictive than the 18-indicator weighted blend.
  • Slow composite is anti-predictive on strategic horizon.
    Slow AUC = 0.486 (below 0.5 = coin flip). High slow-composite stress historically PRECEDES smaller-than-average forward drawdowns — i.e., it's a contrarian buy signal, not a stress-warning. The strategic-tilt copy currently in /#lab is directionally wrong.
  • At GFC peak (2007-10-09), composites were NEGATIVE.
    Fast = -0.43σ, Slow = +0.31σ, All = -0.32σ. The framework signaled LOW stress at the start of the largest drawdown in the data period. Coverage caveat applies (3 of 7 slow indicators didn't exist yet in 2007), but even the fast composite missed it.
  • Slow composite never reaches 'extreme' bucket in 15-year window (2011+).
    Max slow composite value 2011-2026 is +1.02σ. Bucket cutoff for 'extreme' is +1.5σ. The bucket framework is mathematically degenerate — 'strategic extreme' is unreachable.

1. AUC against forward drawdowns

How well does each composite predict whether a forward drawdown will exceed the threshold? AUC = 0.5 is coin flip; AUC > 0.55 is genuine signal; AUC < 0.5 is anti-predictive (mean-reverting).

CompositeTactical AUC
60d, ≤-5% drawdown		nStrategic AUC
252d, ≤-10% drawdown		n
Fast composite (v3)0.55851070.4425107
Slow composite (v3)0.54650920.4865092
COMP — current production0.55851070.4515107
VIX (single indicator)0.59051070.5465107
HY OAS (single indicator)0.42638280.3103828
10Y-2Y (single indicator)0.48451060.5155106
genuine signal (AUC ≥ 0.60) marginal (0.55-0.60) weak (0.52-0.55) noise (0.45-0.52) anti-predictive (< 0.45 — flip the sign for a buy signal)

2. Episode check — did composites flag GFC / 2022 / COVID at the peak?

Composite z-score on the day the S&P put in its cycle peak. If the framework works as intended, fast/slow/all should be elevated (≥ +0.5σ) at peaks that precede major drawdowns.

EpisodePeak dateFast at peakSlow at peakAll at peakSPX 252d drawdown
Dot-com (2000-2002)2000-03-24no data — pre-2006 / sparse
GFC (2007-2009)2007-10-09-0.43σ+0.31σ-0.32σ-37.1%
2022 Rate Shock2022-01-03-0.44σ-0.40σ-0.43σ-25.4%
COVID (2020)2020-02-19-0.28σ+0.02σ-0.20σ-33.9%

Lead time test — composite z-scores 90 days BEFORE each peak

EpisodeT-90 dateFast (90d before peak)SlowAll
Dot-com (2000-2002)1999-12-25no data
GFC (2007-2009)2007-07-11-1.41σ-0.03σ-1.21σ
2022 Rate Shock2021-10-05-0.61σ-0.67σ-0.63σ
COVID (2020)2019-11-21-0.19σ-0.13σ-0.17σ
Coverage caveat for GFC. 3 of 7 slow indicators (cape, ism, bank_unreal) start in 2011 — they didn't exist in our data during the GFC. Only 4 of 7 slow indicators populated the slow composite in 2007-2008. The strategic-tilt result for GFC is therefore biased low. Even with this caveat, the fast composite (which had 16 of 18 inputs available) failed to flag GFC.

3. Forward S&P returns by Fast | Slow bucket combo

For each historical date, classify into one of 16 (Fast, Slow) bucket combinations using the v3 cutoffs (cold < -0.5, neutral [-0.5, 0.5], hot [0.5, 1.5], extreme ≥ 1.5). Mean forward return by combo.

Forward 60d return (tactical horizon)

Fast | Slow bucketnMean fwd 60d returnStd dev
hot | extreme147-8.00%16.21%
neutral | extreme33-5.36%5.91%
neutral | cold460-0.47%6.29%
neutral | neutral2123+1.74%6.65%
extreme | extreme199+2.18%14.48%
cold | neutral582+2.29%4.11%
cold | cold337+3.02%3.39%
hot | neutral269+4.18%7.53%
neutral | hot324+4.73%3.59%
hot | hot394+5.47%5.20%
hot | cold131+7.69%3.97%
extreme | hot36+15.13%3.06%
extreme | neutral12+25.90%7.31%

Forward 252d return (strategic horizon)

Fast | Slow bucketnMean fwd 252d returnStd dev
neutral | extreme33-24.67%18.58%
hot | extreme147-13.45%19.76%
cold | cold337+2.48%11.57%
cold | neutral582+4.51%11.20%
neutral | neutral1963+9.07%13.04%
neutral | cold460+9.30%10.84%
neutral | hot324+9.31%21.46%
hot | neutral237+16.94%11.85%
hot | cold131+18.24%5.35%
hot | hot394+22.47%15.31%
extreme | extreme199+27.00%16.27%
extreme | hot36+47.61%5.80%
extreme | neutral12+60.27%7.53%

Read: The relationship between buckets and forward returns is real but inverted from the v3 punchline copy. "extreme | extreme" (the worst-stress reading) historically precedes a +27% mean 252-day return — classic mean-reversion. "hot | extreme" is the only combo that consistently precedes negative forward returns. The current v3 tilt text says "high readings = de-risk equities" — that's directionally wrong on the strategic horizon.

4. Sector forward 60d return by combo (cross-sectional rotation test)

Mean forward 60-day return per sector ETF, conditional on the (Fast, Slow) combo bucket. Green = above-average, red = below-average. If the v3 framework adds cross-sectional value, sector rankings should shift meaningfully across regimes.

BucketXLBXLCXLEXLFXLIXLKXLPXLREXLUXLVXLY
cold | cold+2.1%-3.7%+4.0%+3.7%+2.2%+4.8%+3.9%+3.7%+3.7%+4.3%+3.3%
cold | neutral+4.1%+5.3%+3.4%+1.9%+3.7%+3.3%+2.0%-2.4%+3.0%+2.2%+2.4%
extreme | extreme+6.0%—+1.4%+3.9%+2.1%+6.8%+1.0%—+1.0%+2.3%+6.2%
extreme | hot+21.2%+18.3%+8.0%+11.3%+17.6%+22.7%+7.4%+9.5%+5.2%+7.7%+22.7%
extreme | neutral+31.2%+30.6%+48.2%+20.1%+24.4%+34.1%+11.8%+21.4%+11.9%+20.0%+36.8%
hot | None———————————
hot | cold+6.3%+13.3%+6.8%+10.0%+9.6%+9.7%+5.9%+17.5%+3.3%+7.1%+9.4%
hot | extreme-11.5%—-9.7%-10.5%-8.3%-8.4%-1.3%—-8.3%-3.7%-6.6%
hot | hot+4.5%+9.6%-0.0%+3.2%+5.8%+10.1%+2.1%+2.5%+0.8%+2.6%+6.6%
hot | neutral+6.9%+1.0%+10.1%+5.1%+7.0%+5.4%+1.9%+0.6%+2.5%+2.4%+2.9%
neutral | None———————————
neutral | cold-1.6%+0.4%-0.0%-1.8%-1.2%+0.1%+1.6%+4.7%+2.2%+2.2%+0.9%
neutral | extreme-7.3%—-9.7%-13.8%-5.4%-3.7%+1.7%—-3.9%+5.2%-3.7%
neutral | hot+4.7%+7.2%+4.2%+5.7%+6.6%+4.5%+3.6%+3.8%+6.1%+1.2%+5.6%
neutral | neutral+1.7%+0.6%+2.2%+1.6%+2.3%+3.0%+2.2%+1.0%+2.4%+2.4%+2.2%

Read: Some signal here — defensive sectors (XLP, XLU, XLV) outperform in stressed combos; cyclicals (XLI, XLB, XLY) outperform in benign combos. But the rotation is dominated by the bucket itself, not by the fast/slow split — the same pattern would emerge from COMP buckets alone.

5. Composite time series

Fast (red) vs slow (blue) vs current COMP (gray dashed) z-scores, with S&P 500 (gray solid, log scale) overlay.

Full coverage (2006-2026)

2011+ subset (full slow-composite coverage)

6. Recommendation

  1. Rip the v3 punchline UI from /#lab. It's misleading as currently calibrated. Strategic tilt copy is directionally wrong (mean-reverting, not stress-warning). Bucket cutoffs are mathematically unreachable for slow.
  2. Drop the equal-weighted-mean composite construction in favor of AUC-weighted single-indicator selection. VIX alone beats the 18-indicator fast composite. The blend is dilution, not aggregation.
  3. Re-evaluate the WEIGHTS table itself. Current WEIGHTS were not derived from predictive AUC against forward drawdowns — they were eyeballed. They should be calibrated using out-of-sample AUC on a rolling basis.
  4. Re-evaluate SD calibration window. Static 15-year mean/SD doesn't reflect regime shifts. A rolling 5-year window would mean the same VIX value scores higher in a calm regime than in a turbulent one — which is what 'stress' actually means.
  5. If the fast/slow framing has UX value, build it on TOP of single-indicator AUC selection. e.g. 'tactical = VIX + HY OAS + MOVE only, AUC-weighted', 'strategic = curve + EBP + ISM only, AUC-weighted'. Composite-of-composites is the wrong abstraction; composite-of-best-indicators-per-horizon is the right one.
  6. Run this back-test with rolling-window SD calibration before recalibrating bucket cutoffs. If the SD basis itself is the bug, fixing it changes all the AUCs. The level-bucket recalibration should happen LAST.

Appendix · Methodology

  • Composite construction mirrors subsetComp_Lab() in src/App.jsx: WEIGHTS-weighted mean of sdScore() z-scores across the indicator subset, skipping nulls. Same calibration tables (SD, WEIGHTS) used in production.
  • Fast subset = 18 indicators with IND_FREQ ∈ {D, W}. Slow subset = 7 indicators with IND_FREQ ∈ {M, Q}.
  • AUC computed via Mann-Whitney U statistic (rank-sum). Forward drawdown = min cumulative return over next N trading days. Threshold: 5% for 60d (tactical), 10% for 252d (strategic).
  • Sector ETFs pulled via yfinance, auto-adjusted close. SPDR sector set: XLB, XLC, XLE, XLF, XLI, XLK, XLP, XLRE, XLU, XLV, XLY.
  • Daily indicator panel built by reindexing each indicator's native frequency to daily and forward-filling. SD calibration uses the static stats block in each indicator's history record (15-year window, 1%-99% winsorized, per __meta__).
